refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation approach) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per the acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and operational areas within the worksite model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model accessible to planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured geometric model (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with semantic labels and metadata; expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign each zone a feature inventory (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as a graph of zone nodes with edge constraints; persist in a queryable schema.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a rule-based symbolic representation (e.g., logic rules or ontology) that encodes spatial relationships, safety constraints, and operational policies; derive a queryable model on demand by evaluating rules against sensor observations.

DP4: Establish a real-time streaming pipeline that continuously ingests sensor data, applies feature extraction and filtering, and maintains an incrementally updated spatial model; expose model state via subscription/polling interface for planning/execution.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to a hybrid approach: use pre-defined templates (e.g., site layout blueprints, CAD models) as a baseline; augment with live sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and transient features; merge into a composite model.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid baseline-augmented model. Establish a composite spatial representation by anchoring to pre-defined worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, or prior surveys) as a static baseline; continuously augment with live multi-modal sensor observations to detect and integrate dynamic features (moving obstacles, temporary hazards, personnel), deviations from baseline, and real-time operational state. Expose the merged model via a structured query interface (spatial queries, feature filters, constraint checks) that supports both planning and execution consumers. Maintain traceability between baseline and observed features to support safety auditing and policy compliance.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor modality selection and feature extraction logic: mitigated by defining a sensor-agnostic feature schema and abstraction layer.
Dependency on baseline template availability and accuracy: mitigated by graceful degradation (operate with live-only model if baseline unavailable) and versioning.
Coupling between model update frequency and planning/execution cycle time: mitigated by decoupling via asynchronous query interface and explicit freshness metadata.
Policy/safety constraint representation tightly bound to model schema: mitigated by externalizing constraints as a separate, versioned policy layer queryable against the model.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves loose coupling by separating concerns: baseline (static, pre-computed), live observations (dynamic, sensor-driven), and query interface (consumer-agnostic). The hybrid approach avoids over-dependence on real-time sensing alone (DP1 risk) or manual annotation brittleness (DP2 risk), and defers rule evaluation overhead (DP3) while supporting incremental updates (DP4 benefit).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior surveys) are available and reasonably accurate at mission start. Risk: If baseline is stale or unavailable, model degrades to live-only; mitigation: define fallback mode and baseline versioning strategy.

Assumption: Multi-modal sensor suite (vision, range, inertial) is available and calibrated. Risk: Sensor failures or miscalibration corrupt feature extraction; mitigation: implement sensor health monitoring and feature confidence scoring.

Assumption: Feature extraction and fusion algorithms can reliably distinguish dynamic hazards from static baseline. Risk: False positives (noise) or false negatives (missed hazards) compromise safety; mitigation: require human-in-the-loop validation for safety-critical features and define confidence thresholds.

Assumption: Query interface latency and model update frequency meet planning/execution cycle time requirements. Risk: Stale model data leads to unsafe or inefficient decisions; mitigation: define SLA for model freshness and implement priority-based update scheduling.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formalized and queryable. Risk: Policies are ambiguous or incomplete; mitigation: require explicit policy schema and validation against model before execution.

Assumption: Baseline and live observations can be reliably registered in a common coordinate frame. Risk: Registration drift or errors propagate through model; mitigation: implement continuous re-registration and drift detection.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 by explicitly defining: (1) the baseline template schema (geometry, feature types, metadata); (2) the live observation schema and confidence/freshness metadata; (3) the merge/reconciliation logic (conflict resolution, priority rules); (4) the query interface contract (spatial predicates, feature filters, constraint checks); (5) the policy layer schema and binding to model features; (6) the fallback/degradation strategy if baseline or sensors fail. Introduce an IM layer to clarify ownership: baseline management (IM-Baseline), live sensor fusion (IM-Fusion), model storage (IM-Store), query service (IM-Query), policy engine (IM-Policy). This decomposition supports independent development, testing, and evolution of each module while maintaining clear interfaces.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial representation engine that maintains hybrid baseline-augmented models, manages sensor fusion, provides structured query interfaces, and tracks feature traceability for safety compliance.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingest multi-modal sensor observations (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to continuously augment baseline model with live dynamic features.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Expose structured spatial query interface (feature filters, constraint checks, spatial queries) to planning and execution consumers (Vehicle autonomy, Teleoperation operator interfaces).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit safety-relevant deviations from baseline, detected hazards, and traceability logs to Safety Services for auditing and policy compliance verification.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receive baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior surveys) and synchronize hybrid model state across distributed nodes if multi-agent coordination required.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE is the natural locus for spatial reasoning, sensor fusion, and model management. Baseline anchoring, augmentation logic, query interface, and traceability tracking all cohere within a single perception/planning backbone. Minor diffusion: SafetyAlert emission requires coordination with Safety Services, but this is a clean interface boundary.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a consumer of the spatial model (for autonomous navigation), not its owner. Placing DP5 here would couple the vehicle controller to baseline management, sensor fusion orchestration, and safety auditing—responsibilities that belong upstream. High diffusion: query interface would need to be re-exposed to Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a consumer interface for remote operation, not a model authority. Placing DP5 here would fragment spatial reasoning across operator workstations, breaking consistency and traceability. High diffusion: baseline management and sensor fusion would need to be duplicated or re-exposed to Vehicle and Safety Services.

Weak fit. Safety Services is a consumer and auditor of the spatial model, not its owner. Placing DP5 here would make Safety Services responsible for real-time sensor fusion and query serving—a violation of separation of concerns. High diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation would need to query Safety Services for operational state, creating latency and coupling issues.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP5 (hybrid baseline-augmented model with sensor fusion, query interface, and traceability) is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) NGHE is the authoritative spatial reasoning backbone; (2) baseline anchoring and augmentation are core perception tasks; (3) the structured query interface naturally serves planning and execution consumers; (4) traceability and safety auditing are upstream responsibilities that feed Safety Services, not the reverse. This placement minimizes diffusion and maintains clean separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: Real-time sensor fusion performance. If NGHE Solution is distributed or latency-sensitive, augmentation delays could degrade safety. Mitigation: Implement local sensor fusion caches on Vehicle with periodic sync to NGHE authority.

Risk: Baseline template management. If blueprints/CAD are managed by external systems (e.g., fleet management, site planning), NGHE must reliably ingest and version them. Mitigation: Define formal baseline ingestion protocol via Network Link interface.

Risk: Query interface scalability. If many consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) issue concurrent spatial queries, NGHE could become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement query caching and asynchronous subscription model for high-frequency consumers.

Risk: Traceability overhead. Maintaining full provenance between baseline and observed features may incur storage and computational cost. Mitigation: Implement selective traceability (e.g., only for safety-critical features) and archival policies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. FR acceptance criteria are concrete: (1) worksite model/map generated and available to planning/execution functions for agreed feature set, (2) alignment with worksite safety plan and operational policies. IM boundary (NGHE Solution) is measurable: model availability can be verified via query interface latency/completeness tests; feature coverage can be audited against baseline templates; traceability logs can be validated against safety policies. Acceptance criteria map directly to IM outputs (Tasking interface for planning/execution consumers, SafetyAlert interface for policy compliance).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of DP5 realization. Responsibilities are well-partitioned: NGHE owns baseline anchoring, sensor fusion orchestration, query serving, and traceability tracking. Vehicle and Teleoperation are consumers (not owners) of the spatial model. Safety Services is an auditor/consumer of traceability logs. This allocation minimizes diffusion and aligns with separation of concerns.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid baseline-augmented model with sensor fusion, structured query interface, and traceability) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable model). DP5 specifies: (1) composite spatial representation via baseline anchoring + live augmentation, (2) multi-modal sensor integration (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU), (3) dynamic feature detection (obstacles, hazards, personnel), (4) structured query interface for planning/execution consumers, (5) traceability for safety auditing. All elements are necessary and sufficient to satisfy FR. No logical gaps or over-specification detected.

diffusion_risks: Sensor fusion distribution risk: If real-time augmentation is latency-critical, centralizing all fusion logic in NGHE Solution could create a bottleneck. Mitigation: Implement local sensor fusion caches on Vehicle subsystem with periodic sync to NGHE authority. Ensure cache invalidation protocol is defined to maintain consistency.

Baseline template ingestion risk: If baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior surveys) are managed by external fleet management or site planning systems, NGHE must reliably ingest, version, and reconcile them. Mitigation: Define formal baseline ingestion protocol via Network Link interface with version control and conflict resolution rules.

Query interface scalability risk: Concurrent spatial queries from Vehicle autonomy, Teleoperation operator interfaces, and Safety Services could saturate NGHE. Mitigation: Implement query result caching, asynchronous subscription model for high-frequency consumers, and query prioritization (safety-critical queries prioritized over planning queries).

Traceability overhead risk: Maintaining full provenance between baseline and observed features (for every dynamic feature, every update cycle) may incur prohibitive storage and computational cost. Mitigation: Implement selective traceability (e.g., only for safety-critical features or anomalies) and archival policies with retention windows.

Cross-module coordination risk: SafetyAlert emission requires coordination with Safety Services. If Safety Services expects real-time alerts but NGHE processes asynchronously, latency could compromise safety. Mitigation: Define alert SLA (e.g., <100ms for hazard detection) and implement priority queue for safety-critical events.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the natural and correct locus for DP5 realization. All core responsibilities (baseline anchoring, sensor fusion, query serving, traceability tracking) cohere within a single perception/planning backbone. External interfaces are clean and well-defined: Telemetry (in) for sensor ingestion, Tasking (out) for query serving, SafetyAlert (out) for compliance, Network Link (inout) for baseline sync. No significant diffusion detected. Alternative placements (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) would fragment responsibilities and create high diffusion (0.65–0.70 scores). Recommended: Proceed with NGHE Solution as owner; implement mitigations for sensor fusion distribution, baseline ingestion, query scalability, and traceability overhead.

recommended_revisions: Clarify sensor fusion latency budget: Define acceptable augmentation delay (e.g., <500ms for dynamic obstacle detection) and specify local caching strategy on Vehicle to meet real-time constraints without centralizing all fusion logic in NGHE.

Formalize baseline ingestion protocol: Document baseline template format (CAD, blueprint, survey data), versioning scheme, conflict resolution rules, and sync frequency via Network Link interface. Include acceptance criteria for baseline completeness and accuracy.

Specify query interface SLA: Define response time targets for spatial queries (e.g., <100ms for feature lookup, <1s for constraint checks) and implement caching/subscription model to prevent NGHE bottleneck.

Define traceability scope and retention: Specify which features require full provenance tracking (e.g., safety-critical hazards only) and archival policy (e.g., retain traceability logs for 30 days, then summarize). Quantify storage/compute overhead.
Establish SafetyAlert SLA: Define alert latency requirement (e.g., <100ms for hazard detection) and priority queue discipline to ensure safety-critical events are not delayed by planning queries.

Add IM interface specification: Document Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces with message schemas, frequency, and error handling to enable integration testing and validation against acceptance criteria.

decision.justification: FR5 (Capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable model) passes all three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) with high verification feasibility and clear allocation. However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that DP5 is logically sound but operationally underspecified. The safety-critical nature of spatial modeling (hazard detection, obstacle avoidance, personnel tracking) and the identified latency/scalability/traceability concerns warrant decomposition into sub-FRs to address: (1) real-time sensor fusion with bounded latency, (2) baseline model ingestion and versioning, (3) query interface performance and scalability, (4) safety-critical traceability and alert coordination. Decomposition will force explicit design of these concerns as first-class FRs rather than mitigations, enabling rigorous verification and risk containment at lower levels.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Fuse multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU) into a unified spatial representation with latency ≤500ms for dynamic feature augmentation.
FR5.2: Ingest, version, and reconcile baseline spatial templates (CAD, blueprints, prior surveys) from external fleet management systems with conflict resolution and completeness validation.

FR5.3: Serve spatial feature queries (lookup, constraint checks, proximity analysis) to Vehicle autonomy, Teleoperation, and Safety Services with response latency ≤100ms (lookup) and ≤1s (constraint checks).
FR5.4: Track and audit provenance of safety-critical spatial features (hazards, obstacles, personnel) with selective traceability and archival policy (30-day retention, then summarization).
FR5.5: Detect and emit safety-critical spatial anomalies (new hazards, personnel in exclusion zones, sensor degradation) to Safety Services with alert latency ≤100ms and priority queue discipline.

revise.why: DP5 is logically correct but requires refinement to address latency budgets, caching strategies, and interface SLAs. Decomposition will force explicit DPs for each sub-FR (e.g., DP5.1: local sensor fusion caches with periodic sync; DP5.2: formal baseline ingestion protocol with versioning; DP5.3: query result caching + subscription model; DP5.4: selective traceability with archival; DP5.5: priority queue for safety alerts). This will enable verification of latency, scalability, and safety compliance at lower levels and reduce risk of bottlenecks or alert delays in production.

assumptions: Sensor fusion latency budget of 500ms is acceptable for dynamic obstacle detection; faster augmentation (e.g., <100ms) may require local caching on Vehicle subsystem.
Baseline templates are available from external fleet management systems via Network Link interface with documented format and versioning scheme.
Query interface can be scaled via caching and subscription model without requiring distributed fusion logic across Vehicle subsystem.
Safety-critical features (hazards, personnel) can be identified deterministically and tracked selectively without full provenance overhead.
Safety Services can consume SafetyAlert events asynchronously with <100ms latency SLA; real-time synchronous coordination is not required.
NGHE Solution has sufficient compute and storage capacity to support sensor fusion, query serving, and traceability tracking without architectural redesign.
open_risks: Sensor fusion distribution: If local caching on Vehicle creates stale data, cache invalidation protocol must be robust to prevent safety-critical misalignment between Vehicle autonomy and NGHE authority model.
Baseline ingestion conflicts: If external baseline templates conflict with observed features, conflict resolution rules must be safety-aware (e.g., observed hazards override baseline assumptions).

Query bottleneck: If concurrent queries from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services exceed NGHE throughput, query prioritization discipline must be enforced to prevent safety-critical queries from being delayed.
Traceability overhead: If selective traceability is insufficient for safety auditing, full provenance tracking may become necessary, incurring prohibitive storage/compute cost.
Alert latency: If SafetyAlert emission is delayed by NGHE processing load, hazard detection may not meet <100ms SLA, compromising safety.
Integration testing: Latency, scalability, and safety SLAs cannot be verified without end-to-end integration testing across Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services, and NGHE Solution.
evidence_needed: Sensor fusion latency measurements: Benchmark augmentation delay for LiDAR, camera, radar, IMU fusion under nominal and peak load conditions. Validate <500ms budget and local caching strategy.

Baseline ingestion protocol specification: Document baseline template format, versioning scheme, conflict resolution rules, and sync frequency. Validate against external fleet management system interfaces.
Query interface performance testing: Measure response latency for feature lookup, constraint checks, and proximity analysis under concurrent load. Validate <100ms (lookup) and <1s (constraint) SLAs.
Traceability overhead quantification: Measure storage and compute cost of full vs. selective provenance tracking. Validate archival policy (30-day retention) is sufficient for safety auditing.
SafetyAlert SLA validation: Measure alert latency from hazard detection to Safety Services notification under nominal and peak load. Validate <100ms SLA and priority queue discipline.

Integration test plan: Define end-to-end test scenarios covering sensor fusion, baseline ingestion, query serving, traceability, and alert emission. Include safety-critical failure modes (sensor degradation, baseline conflicts, query saturation).
